does: This paper introduces Rule-Based Rewards, a method that automatically generates reward signals to steer model behavior toward safety without requiring large-scale human annotation. The approach formulates safety constraints as explicit rules that can be evaluated programmatically, converting compliance with these rules into reward signals during training. The method is applied to align language models or other AI systems, demonstrating that RBRs can effectively enforce safe behavior patterns across various contexts while maintaining model utility. The core contribution is showing that declaratively specified safety rules can substitute for expensive human preference data in reward model training, reducing the data bottleneck in alignment work.
connects: This work extends the reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) paradigm by replacing or reducing dependence on human preference annotations with automatically computable rule-based signals. It builds on prior research in AI alignment, particularly methods that use reward models to guide model behavior, and connects to broader efforts in AI safety that seek to specify constraints or objectives more directly rather than through learning from human examples. The approach also relates to work on constraint-based optimization and symbolic specification of safety properties, drawing on the insight that many safety requirements can be expressed as formal or semi-formal rules. It responds to the practical challenge that human data collection for alignment is expensive, slow, and potentially inconsistent.
enables: This framework opens paths toward more scalable alignment methods that could reduce reliance on human feedback for safety training. It suggests future work on automatically inferring or generating rule sets from high-level safety objectives, composing multiple rule-based reward signals, and extending RBRs to more complex safety properties that resist simple programmatic evaluation. The approach could enable faster iteration on model safety improvements and allow organizations with clear safety specifications but limited annotation budgets to deploy safer systems. It also points toward hybrid methods combining rule-based rewards with human feedback, and raises questions about how to systematically discover or validate rules that truly capture safety goals without unintended side effects.
